text: A 3-year-old child with a painful elbow held in pronation after a traction injury. Diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Nursemaid's Elbow (radial head subluxation) - occurs after pulling on child's arm.

text: Trauma patient with pelvic fractures, blood at the meatus, and a high-riding prostate. Next step?
rationale: Dx: Urethral injury. Retrograde urethrography before any catheter attempt.

text: A 45-year-old obese male... 3-3-2 rule. What does the "2" evaluate?
rationale: 3-3-2 rule: thyroid-hyoid distance (2 fingers).

text: Surgeon performs elective cholecystectomy. Minor liver laceration repaired without harm. Disclosure obligation?
rationale: Canadian ethics: Disclose adverse events + apology (not legal admission).

text: 6yo in hemorrhagic shock. Jehovah's Witness parents refuse transfusion. Next step?
rationale: Best interests of child override parental religious refusal in life-threatening emergency.

text: A 6-year-old child is brought to the emergency department in hemorrhagic shock following a severe motor vehicle collision. The trauma team determines that an immediate, life-saving blood transfusion is required. The child's parents, who are devout Jehovah's Witnesses, firmly refuse the blood transfusion on religious grounds. According to Canadian medical ethics and legal standards, what is the most appropriate next step for the physician?
options: Respect the parents' autonomy and withhold the blood transfusion, providing alternative volume expanders only.
Administer the life-saving blood transfusion, as the physician has a strong obligation to act in the child's best interests.
Delay the transfusion until a court order can be obtained from a judge.
Ask the 6-year-old child for their assent before proceeding with the transfusion.
rationale: SDMs (parents) must act in the child's BEST INTERESTS, not based on religious beliefs. In life-threatening emergencies, physicians must administer life-saving treatment even against parental refusal. Child welfare authorities are notified concurrently.

text: A 58-year-old male with BMI 38 kg/m² and poorly controlled hypertension is evaluated preoperatively. His wife reports loud snoring and gasping for air during sleep. Using the STOP-BANG questionnaire, what condition is this patient at highest risk for?
rationale: STOP-BANG: Snoring ✓, Observed apnea ✓, Pressure/HTN ✓, BMI>35 ✓, Age>50 ✓, Male ✓ = High risk OSA. OSA increases risk of difficult ventilation and postoperative airway obstruction.

text: A 72-year-old male with progressive exertional dyspnea. ECG: QRS 145 msec, broad/notched R waves in leads I, aVL, V5, V6, and deep S waves in V1-V2. No Q waves in lateral leads. What is the correct interpretation?
rationale: LBBB criteria: QRS >120ms + broad/notched R waves in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5, V6) + deep S waves in V1-V2. RBBB = 'rabbit ear' rsR pattern in V1. New LBBB in chest pain = treat as STEMI equivalent (Sgarbossa criteria).

text: A 26-year-old pregnant patient (14 weeks) with IV substance use disorder is admitted with infective endocarditis. She frequently leaves AMA to use illicit substances. Can she be legally detained to protect the fetus?
options: She can be detained under the Mental Health Act to protect the fetus.
The fetus has independent legal rights overriding maternal autonomy after 12 weeks.
She cannot be detained or treated against her will to protect the fetus, as the fetus has no legal rights until born alive.
The physician can obtain a court order to mandate her admission until delivery.
rationale: Under Canadian law, a fetus has NO legal rights until born alive (Criminal Code). A pregnant woman with capacity CANNOT be detained/forced to undergo treatment solely to protect the fetus. Maternal autonomy takes full precedence.

text: A 30-year-old male requires emergency laparotomy after stab wound. BP 75/40 mmHg (hypovolemic shock) with severe active asthma. Which IV induction agent is most appropriate for RSI?
rationale: Ketamine: 1) Sympathomimetic → raises BP/HR → ideal for hypovolemic shock. 2) Bronchodilator → ideal for severe asthma. Propofol and Thiopental cause vasodilation/myocardial depression → dangerous in shock.

text: A 65-year-old collapses in telemetry unit. Unresponsive, no pulse. Monitor shows Ventricular Fibrillation (VF). CPR initiated. According to ACLS guidelines, what is the most critical immediate next step?
options: Immediate delivery of a biphasic defibrillation shock
rationale: VF/pVT = shockable rhythms → IMMEDIATE defibrillation is priority. CPR continues while charging. Epinephrine (every 3-5 min) and Amiodarone (300mg after 3rd shock) come later if rhythm remains refractory.

text: A 35-year-old female receives spinal anesthetic for C-section. 10 minutes later: nausea, dyspnea, BP 80/45, HR 45. Sensory block at T2 level. Primary pathophysiological mechanism?
options: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity affecting the cerebral cortex
Anaphylactic reaction to the local anesthetic
Vagal nerve stimulation from surgical incision
rationale: High Spinal: anesthetic ascends to T1-T4 → blocks cardiac accelerator sympathetic fibers → unopposed vagal tone → bradycardia + hypotension. Tx: IV fluids, Ephedrine, Atropine, left lateral decubitus position.

text: A 75-year-old competent male with terminal pancreatic cancer requests MAID. Unbearable pain not relieved by palliative care. Meets all criteria. Under Canadian legislation, how many independent medical assessments are required?
options: None, if the patient has a written directive.
rationale: MAID requires TWO independent assessors (physician or NP) who must both independently confirm all eligibility criteria: natural death reasonably foreseeable, grievous/irremediable condition, enduring intolerable suffering, capable voluntary consent.

text: A 45-year-old female with anorexia nervosa and chronic diarrhea has muscle cramps and palpitations. ECG: QTc 540 msec with normal T waves. Which electrolyte abnormality is most likely?
rationale: Hypocalcemia prolongs phase 2 of cardiac action potential → prolonged ST segment → prolonged QT. Mnemonic: HypoCa = long QT. Hypercalcemia = short QT. Hyperkalemia = peaked T waves + wide QRS.

text: A 70 kg adult patient requires maintenance IV fluids during lengthy elective surgery (NPO). Using the 4-2-1 rule, what is the correct hourly infusion rate?
rationale: 4-2-1 Rule: First 10kg = 4×10=40 mL/hr. Next 10kg = 2×10=20 mL/hr. Remaining 50kg = 1×50=50 mL/hr. Total = 40+20+50 = 110 mL/hr for 70kg patient.

text: A 65-year-old male presents confused, diaphoretic, BP 80/50 mmHg. Cardiac monitor shows sinus bradycardia at 35 bpm. According to ACLS guidelines, what is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological intervention?
rationale: Symptomatic bradycardia (hypotension + AMS) → First-line: IV Atropine 1mg. If ineffective → transcutaneous pacing OR dopamine/epinephrine infusion.

text: A First Nations child on a reserve requires a specialized medical device for a chronic condition. Jurisdictional dispute between provincial and federal governments threatens to delay the child's care. Which legal obligation mandates prompt equitable access to the device while the payment dispute is resolved later?
options: The Truth and Reconciliation Commission Calls to Action
rationale: Jordan's Principle: First Nations children get prompt equitable access to healthcare. Care is provided FIRST, payment dispute resolved LATER.

text: A 55-year-old ICU patient becomes unresponsive. Monitor shows asystole (flat line). No palpable pulse. High-quality CPR initiated. According to ACLS Adult Cardiac Arrest Algorithm, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Administer IV Epinephrine as soon as possible
rationale: Asystole and PEA = NON-shockable rhythms. For non-shockable rhythms continue high-quality CPR + IV Epinephrine ASAP + treat reversible causes.

text: A 40-year-old female scheduled for elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The anesthesiologist evaluates her PONV risk. Which combination represents recognized independent clinical risk factors for PONV?
options: Male gender, current smoker, history of motion sickness
Female gender, non-smoker, history of PONV
Female gender, current smoker, use of regional anesthesia
rationale: Apfel PONV Risk Factors: female gender, non-smoker, history of PONV or motion sickness, and postoperative opioid use.

text: A 58-year-old male with acute severe retrosternal chest pain. ECG: ST elevation in leads I, aVL, V5, V6 with reciprocal ST depression in inferior leads. Which coronary artery territory is most likely involved?
rationale: ST elevation I, aVL, V5, V6 = Lateral STEMI = Left Circumflex artery.

text: A 72-year-old male with severe COPD exacerbation requires intubation to survive. Full capacity assessment confirms he understands his condition and that refusal means death. He firmly refuses intubation. Most appropriate action?
options: Intubate the patient under beneficence, as refusal leads to death.
Contact his SDM to override the patient's refusal.
Respect the patient's autonomy and withhold intubation, providing palliative symptom management.
Request emergency psychiatric assessment to force life-saving treatment.
rationale: Capable patient has the right to refuse any treatment, even life-saving. Provide palliative care and document the capacity assessment.

text: During preoperative airway assessment, patient opens mouth maximally and protrudes tongue. Anesthesiologist can visualize hard palate and base of uvula only. Tonsillar pillars entirely obscured. What is the Mallampati classification?
rationale: Mallampati Class III = soft palate and base of uvula only.

text: A 32-year-old female with sudden palpitations and lightheadedness. BP stable 120/80 mmHg. Monitor: regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 180 bpm. Vagal maneuvers failed. According to ACLS, what is the most appropriate next pharmacological intervention?
rationale: Stable narrow-complex tachycardia after failed vagal maneuvers is treated with rapid IV adenosine.

text: A family physician is invited to a pharmaceutical company-sponsored 'lunch and learn' about a new antihypertensive. The company offers to pay travel expenses and provides a free lunch. According to Canadian ethical guidelines, what is the most appropriate action?
options: Accept travel and lunch if the physician agrees to prescribe the medication to appropriate patients.
Decline the sponsorship for travel expenses, as it is inappropriate unless the physician is a presenter.
Accept the lunch and travel but disclose this to all patients receiving the medication.
Allow the company to review prescribing data in exchange for the benefits.
rationale: Travel sponsorship to attend industry events is inappropriate unless the physician is participating in a legitimate professional role such as presenter.

text: A 29-year-old postpartum patient who received epidural for labor analgesia reports severe headache on day 2. Headache worsens significantly when sitting/standing and almost completely resolves when lying flat. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Post-dural puncture headache is classically postural and occurs after dural puncture from spinal or epidural procedures.

text: In a randomized controlled trial, the case fatality rate was lower in patients who received a drug. The result reported P = 0.05. What does this mean?
options: Treated patients were 20 times less likely to die
The probability that the observed difference occurred by chance alone is 1 in 20
The same result will occur in 19 out of 20 trials
Ninety-five percent of treated patients survived
rationale: P=0.05 means there is a 5% probability the observed difference occurred by chance alone if the null hypothesis were true.

text: A 25-year-old man presents with 2 months of knee swelling and pain, plus 2 years of low back pain and morning stiffness. Which investigation would most likely confirm the diagnosis?
rationale: Ankylosing spondylitis is supported by sacroiliitis on X-ray or MRI of the sacroiliac joints.

text: A woman is found unconscious in her car in a closed garage. Her PaO2 is normal, but pulse oximetry shows low oxygen saturation. What explains this discrepancy?
rationale: Carbon monoxide poisoning can present with normal PaO2 because dissolved oxygen is unaffected, while hemoglobin is occupied by carbon monoxide.

text: A 77-year-old man reports urinary frequency, nocturia, and feeling that his bladder is always full, with leakage of urine day and night. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Overflow incontinence presents with chronic retention, a full bladder, and continuous leakage, often from BPH in older men.

text: An 8-year-old boy has a persistent barking cough for 6 months. His mother reports frequent blinking and head shaking movements. Which aspect of the patient's history is most important?
rationale: Tourette syndrome commonly coexists with ADHD and learning issues, so the effect on school and academic performance is important.

text: A woman reports physical abuse by her husband. A police officer requests medical information for the investigation. What is the best next step?
options: Review the patient's file and speak with police
Contact the patient to discuss the request
Send a medical report to police
rationale: Patient consent should be sought before releasing information to police unless a specific legal exception applies.

text: A pregnant woman with BMI 58 asks how much weight she should gain during pregnancy. What is the best advice?
options: Weight gain should be based on prepregnancy BMI
rationale: Recommended gestational weight gain depends on prepregnancy BMI, and patients with obesity should generally gain less than those with normal BMI.

text: A patient taking paroxetine develops anejaculation and wants to switch antidepressants. Which medication is most appropriate?
rationale: Bupropion is less likely than SSRIs or SNRIs to cause sexual dysfunction.

text: A patient reports: unstable relationships, fear of abandonment, emotional instability, and chronic feelings of emptiness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Borderline personality disorder features unstable relationships, abandonment fear, affective instability, and chronic emptiness.

text: A teacher believes coworkers are sending secret messages about him through hand signals. He has no hallucinations and maintains normal functioning. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Delusional disorder features fixed delusions with relatively preserved functioning and without the broader syndrome of schizophrenia.

text: A patient receives a penicillin injection and 30 minutes later develops: BP 70/30, HR 130, wheezing, and flushed skin. What is the most appropriate treatment?
rationale: The first-line treatment for anaphylaxis is intramuscular epinephrine.

text: In a randomized controlled trial evaluating a new drug, the mortality rate in the treated group is lower than in the control group. The result is reported as P = 0.05. What does this indicate?
options: The difference observed could have occurred by chance with a probability of 5%
The treatment will work in 19 of 20 patients
rationale: P=0.05 indicates a 5% probability that the difference observed could have occurred by chance.

text: A 25-year-old man presents with knee swelling and pain for 2 months plus chronic back pain and morning stiffness for 2 years. Which investigation confirms the diagnosis?
rationale: Sacroiliac joint imaging supports the diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis.

text: A patient is found unconscious in a car inside a closed garage. Arterial oxygen pressure (PaO2) is normal, but pulse oximetry shows low oxygen saturation. Most likely cause?
rationale: Carbon monoxide poisoning should be suspected when exposure history is typical and oxygen measurements appear discordant.

text: A 77-year-old man reports urinary frequency, nocturia, persistent bladder fullness with leakage. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Overflow incontinence often results from chronic retention with incomplete bladder emptying.

text: An 8-year-old boy has frequent blinking, head shaking, and repeated coughing sounds for several months. Which condition is most likely?
rationale: Tourette syndrome requires both motor and vocal tics with onset before age 18.

text: A woman reports physical abuse by her partner. Police request medical records. What is the best next step?
options: Transfer the case to social services
rationale: Confidentiality generally requires patient consent before disclosure unless there is a defined legal exception.

text: A pregnant woman with BMI 58 asks about recommended weight gain during pregnancy. What is the best recommendation?
options: Base weight gain on prepregnancy BMI
rationale: Gestational weight gain targets are determined by prepregnancy BMI.

text: A patient taking paroxetine develops sexual dysfunction. Which antidepressant is least likely to cause this side effect?
rationale: Bupropion is among the antidepressants least associated with sexual side effects.

text: A patient reports fear of abandonment, unstable relationships, impulsivity, and emotional instability. Diagnosis?
rationale: Borderline personality disorder is characterized by instability in relationships, self-image, and affect, with marked impulsivity.

text: A teacher believes coworkers communicate secret messages about him using hand gestures. No hallucinations, normal functioning. Diagnosis?
rationale: Delusional disorder involves persistent delusions with otherwise preserved functioning.

text: A child develops fever, drooling, and stridor. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Epiglottitis classically causes fever, drooling, distress, and stridor and is an airway emergency.

text: A 4-month-old infant develops cough and wheezing after a viral upper respiratory infection. Most likely cause?
rationale: RSV is the most common cause of bronchiolitis in infants.

text: A patient develops hypotension, wheezing, and rash after penicillin injection. Best treatment?
rationale: Epinephrine is the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis.

text: A patient with severe pancreatitis develops dyspnea and dullness to percussion on the left chest. Most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Severe pancreatitis can cause a reactive pleural effusion, often left sided.

text: A trauma patient develops elevated creatinine after a crush injury. Most likely cause?
rationale: Crush injury can cause rhabdomyolysis with myoglobin-mediated acute kidney injury.

text: A patient taking alprazolam daily stops suddenly and develops tremor, anxiety, sweating, and agitation. Best treatment?
rationale: Benzodiazepine withdrawal is treated by restarting a benzodiazepine and tapering gradually.

text: A pregnant woman with hypertension, proteinuria, headache, and epigastric pain presents at 38 weeks. Which test should be included in evaluation for HELLP syndrome?
rationale: HELLP evaluation includes LDH, liver enzymes, platelet count, and hemolysis markers.

text: A 5-year-old child snores loudly and has daytime fatigue. Which test confirms the diagnosis of obstructive sleep apnea?
rationale: Polysomnography is the gold standard test for obstructive sleep apnea.

text: A patient with schizoaffective disorder gains 15 kg after starting an atypical antipsychotic. Best management?
options: Switch to another atypical antipsychotic with fewer metabolic effects
rationale: Switching to an antipsychotic with fewer metabolic effects is often appropriate when weight gain is clinically significant.

text: A patient with ALS has severe pain not controlled by other treatments. Morphine is prescribed. The patient later dies. What is this considered?
rationale: This is palliative symptom control under the principle of double effect, not assisted suicide.

text: A 9-month-old infant is not crawling, can sit only with support, and has mild head lag when pulled to sitting. What is the best next step?
options: Refer to a pediatrician for developmental assessment
rationale: Head lag and motor delay at 9 months are red flags that warrant developmental assessment.

text: A 5-year-old boy repeatedly passes stool in his clothes. Examination shows a firm mass in the left lower quadrant. Most appropriate management?
rationale: Encopresis from constipation is treated with disimpaction and maintenance laxatives plus bowel routine.

text: A physician studies dermatitis cases among factory workers using workplace records. What type of study is this?
rationale: Descriptive observational studies describe disease patterns without assigning exposure or intervention.

text: A child is extremely aggressive, steals, lies, and harms animals. What medication may help control aggression?
rationale: Risperidone may help severe aggression in disruptive behavior disorders when indicated.

text: A pregnant woman at 30 weeks presents with vaginal bleeding and severe back pain. Best initial step?
rationale: Painful bleeding in late pregnancy suggests placental abruption, and maternal stabilization with IV access and fluids is the first priority.

text: A 23-year-old man presents with periumbilical pain migrating to the right lower quadrant with anorexia and localized rebound tenderness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
rationale: Dx: Acute appendicitis. In Canadian-style emergency general surgery practice, appendectomy remains standard treatment for uncomplicated adult appendicitis, with early surgical assessment and imaging used selectively.
